flashcards.back: A searching algorithm that sequentially checks each element in a list until the target is found or all elements are checked.

flashcards.front: What is the worst-case time complexity of Linear Search?
flashcards.back: O(n), where n is the number of elements in the array.

flashcards.front: What is the best-case time complexity of Linear Search?
flashcards.back: O(1), when the target element is at the first position.

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of Linear Search?
flashcards.back: O(1), as it requires only constant additional space.

flashcards.front: Does Linear Search require the array to be sorted?
flashcards.back: No, Linear Search works on both sorted and unsorted arrays.

flashcards.front: What value is typically returned when the target element is not found?
How many comparisons does Linear Search make on average?
flashcards.back: Approximately n/2 comparisons when the element is present in the array.

flashcards.front: What is a key advantage of Linear Search?
flashcards.back: It works on unsorted data and requires no preprocessing.

flashcards.front: What is a main disadvantage of Linear Search?
flashcards.back: It becomes inefficient for large datasets due to O(n) time complexity.
An optimization that places the target element at the end to eliminate boundary checks in each iteration.
